sentences.text: Things became worse in Kaukehmen.
My father and my mother, they had to give up their business, and they had to get out.
And we always felt, after, that East Prussia was the birthplace of anti-Semitism.
We always had felt it, after a while.
And so my sister and I, and another sister from [NON-ENGLISH], Dora, who joined us in Berlin, we insisted that my parents come to Berlin.
And we shared an apartment with them.
And they brought along Ruth and Lea.
They were the youngest.
And Lea was only-- she was very, very young.
And so Lea was about 14.
It wasn't long after, we had an invitation from the Jewish organization to attend a meeting where Mrs.
Henrietta Szold, ?] from Israel, and Dr.
Martin Buber were the speakers.
And we went.
My mother and I went.
And the-- it was that they encouraged parents with children 15 and below to let them go to Israel, because they saw the handwriting on the wall.
And they wanted to save these children from the terror of Adolf Hitler.
Yes, we first didn't.
It wasn't so much that we didn't, but it was so overwhelming.
And for my parents to let my little, youngest sister of 15 go to Israel all by herself-- they were overwhelmed.
But we talked it over with the family, and we all decided, because for their safety and for their life, we let them go.
That must have been about 1934-- yes, 1934, 1935.
In the meantime, my brother Paul, who had his own business in Vienna, felt anti-Semitism there, gave up his store, and went to a [HEBREW] in Denmark, to migrate to Israel.
The other brother, who also had a tremendous job already in a-- in a business in East Prussia, [NON-ENGLISH], also felt the pressure.
And he went to a [HEBREW], because you couldn't get into Israel unless you were able or wanting to work for a farm.
So they-- he went, and he left for Israel.
So when, then, came that we were going to send Lea, the youngest, to Israel, my mother felt, perhaps the boys will look after her.
But Mrs.
Henrietta Szold ?] took her and hundreds of children with her.
And my mother, and I, and my father went to the railroad station to see them off.
My mother almost collapsed.
It was a terrible thing.
I was there, yes.
And so she went.
And--
Oh, I was-- I must have been in the 20th, in the 20th.
And so in the meantime, I still worked for that brewery industry.
And we were very honored with the brewery industry.
We had to go to the companies and taste all their beers, because we controlled the beers.
And we had to-- and we had to be-- we had to be there all the time.
But my eyes and ears always were open.
And finally, finally the big boss called me in.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH], we cannot keep you anymore, because the government wants to have their hands in our business.
And we have to give them the names of every employee and every background, and we know you are Jewish.
And you wouldn't last.
So they gave me six month wages to cover me until I found another job.
That must have been in 1935.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had to work for two more weeks there, to work-- help a young girl come in, take over my job.
She was raised and trained in the Hitler Youth group, and she was all Hitler.
At lunch time, we would have lunch in the kitchen.
We would bring our own lunch.
And when Hitler spoke, my boss would say, turn off the radio.
She would come, and she would tell my big boss, Hitler means more to her than my boss.
And we have to listen to him.
They couldn't do anything about it, because they were afraid for this little girl, that was a nothing, to report them to the Gestapo.

So finally, a friend of mine who worked in a [NON-ENGLISH] from Czechoslovakia, their branch in Berlin, and she left Germany from one day to another, and left her position in that company for America.
She migrated to America.
So she gave me the name.
And she said, you go there, and you take over my job, which I did.
It didn't take me more than two weeks, then find the job.
And I worked in the foreign department.
We delivered all the pharmaceutical things to pharmacists all over the world.
And-- and we-- we had a very important man-- he was a professor-- who was my boss.
And Dr.
Mazuer was my boss, and we worked very well.
In the meantime, I got married.
I met my husband.
Where did you meet him?
My husband, I met through my father, his cousin, and my husband's aunt and uncle.
They used to play Skat.
And my--
Skat was a special card game.
And my father would play Skat with a-- with Hugo's uncle, Dave [?
Hamburger, ?] and my father's cousin.
And he-- I didn't know him.
But Hugo, at the mean time-- I will say, if I may-- he used to work for his uncle in a big bank.
And they had a foreign investment group.
Now, Hugo, at that time-- I didn't know-- was trained in the jewelry business, in [GERMAN], near Frankfurt am Main.
And this uncle felt that someone was cheating in the investment department.
And he wanted somebody of his family to oversee.
So they-- he went to the Hugo's parents and begged them to let Hugo come to them.
And they were multi-multi-millionaires.
They treated Hugo like anything you have never heard of.
When they went to the opera, they would have a tailor come and make him a formal attire in one day, so he could attend the opera.
Anyhow, they lost the bank because of the Black Friday.
And they lost everything.
So Hugo was very loyal to them, and roomed with them.
And that's when my father went to play Skat.
And at that time, Hugo had taken a job in a big factory, who made suitcases and handbags.
And he wanted to stay in Berlin.
So my-- and he traveled quite extensively.
And when Hugo came home, he stayed with his aunt and uncle.
And my father met him, and my father fell in love with him.
Then my father arranged for his family to come to our home.
And his aunt fell in love with me.
So when Hugo came back the next time, from his trip, they arranged to have a Skat game in our home.
And Hugo was invited.
But we were six sisters, and he didn't know who was who.
In the meantime, I had a date that night, and I didn't come home until about 11:00 PM.
But they were all there.
Hugo excused himself from the Skat game, and they danced.
We had another room where they danced and had a wonderful time.
And when I came in, he asked me to dance and immediately asked me for a date.
And that's how I got to know Hugo.
He was a very, very fine man, very respectful, and a very fine man.
Anyhow, we courted for one year, got engaged.
And then another year, we got married.
We got married on the 9th of August, of 1936.
At that time, I still worked in the brewery industry.
And it was customary that they are preparing for-- that they are giving a present to anyone who marries.
And so they asked me what I wanted.
And I-- because that was the custom.
And I told them I wanted a Persian rug, which I got.
And when-- at our wedding, already, all the big bosses came, disguised, to temple Fasanenstrasse.
We were married by Dr.
Levondovski in Berlin, at the Fasanenstrasse synagogue.
They already didn't want to-- to have anyone know that they are Germans, because already, at that time, they were afraid to mingle with Germans.
They didn't want to know, or they didn't want anyone to recognize them, that they were non-Jewish.
They came disguised.
They had dark glasses, and they had hats on.
And so no one really could know.
At that time, I was still working for the brewery industry.
And when I got home, I got presents from everyone in the brewery industry, extra presents, which was very unusual.
My home-- my parents' home, rather, I lived with my parents-- was like a flower shop.
Bouquets, and bouquets, and bouquets, and plants from people who I worked with and had some connection with-- so they were wonderful.
So anyhow, in the meantime, I had to change jobs, as I said before.
And I worked, then, for the [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was-- suddenly, we heard that there was a lot of turmoil.
In the meantime, I was having Sonia, my daughter.
She was born 1938, on the 10th of April.
My husband lost his job at the day when she was born.
I had given up my job, because I wanted to take care of my baby.
So I couldn't-- I couldn't be without the job.
I needed the money.
So my husband went to his family in Dusseldorf, that they had moved to with their jewelry store, and wanted to get re-acquainted in the jewelry business.
He had learned to be a watchmaker, also.
And he was preparing, eventually, for us to leave Germany.
And he had to have a profession.
Because we noticed that there was nothing there for us, because my husband lost a job.
He couldn't get another job.
And there were not too many foreign companies that would employ a Jewish people.
So we felt that we had to think.
I will say this.
Before I met my husband-- this is important.
I met a non-Jewish man, a young man, who traveled worldwide.
And when he came back, he told me-- he said, Irmie, I want you to study English.
You will not be able to live in Germany.
You should be prepared.
Our dates were in restaurants, where he would teach me English.
When he left, he gave me a name of a woman-- an elderly woman-- that taught English.
Her name was Professor Schafer.
And I went to her, and she taught English in a very unusual way.
She didn't go with grammar.
She went with stories, and the story was the-- the-- an animal caught in a bottle-- the bottle ink.
And that's how I learned English.
I never saw that man again, but I was grateful that he suggested that I should know English, because apparently-- who he was, I never found out.
But he must have known something.
So in the meantime, my husband went to Dusseldorf to stay with his parents, and work there a little bit, and get re-acquainted.
That was about in October of 1938.
One month later, on the 10th of November, we had the Kristallnacht.
Where were you at that time?
Working for the Czechoslovakian firm in Berlin.
My mother, because I had to give up my home and moved in with my mother, because I had to work.
My husband was not, anymore, a breadwinner.
So I moved in with my-- with my parents, at the [GERMAN] in Berlin.
So when I heard, over the radio, turmoil, turmoil, turmoil-- when I walked home, I had the worst experience in life.
The Nazis pulled out people, from their homes.
Stores-- plundered the stores, people helped themselves to whatever they wanted.
And what they didn't want, they threw on the-- on the ground.
What did you see?
I saw dead bodies.
I walked over dead bodies.
I saw-- I passed our synagogue.
I saw Torahs.
I saw all holy objects, partially broken, partially stepped on.
It was the most horrible experience I had in my life.
And people were rejoicing, dancing in the streets, to see the dead bodies of Jewish people.
When, then, I--
I was frightened.
I was frightened.
And the only reason that they didn't touch our office was because it was a Czechoslovakian company.
And they were not allowed to touch it.
Hitler would not let them touch any foreign companies, and that's why I was protected.
Here, again, I was the only Jewish girl working.
And my boss, Dr.
Mazuer, was Jewish.
And I think the big boss was Jewish.
He had only the foreign department.
Anyhow, when I got home from the brewery industry, the big bosses came to my home, again, disguised-- brought me food and clothes for the baby.
And they admitted that this was their fault, because they did not speak up.
They let him roam.
They didn't give him credence.
They felt he was a crazy man, and therefore they should have done something.
But now it's too late.
And they apologized.
To me-- they said, [?
Roshan, ?] would we have taken him serious, we would not have let him become our fuhrer.
And they were devastated.
I also want to mention, which I didn't before, while I was working in the brewery industry, one of the ladies came to the-- to our office, crying bitterly.
Why?
They had come and picked up her sister and brother-in-law because their daughter was indoctrinated in the youth group.
And they were told, if any of all of them hear their parents speak ill about Hitler, to report it.
They are going to be made heroes.
And this little girl reported her parents, and they picked them up.
Now, in my cases, they let them go with a warning.
In-- in severe cases, they shot them.
So I just thought I'd let you in on that.
So anyhow, in the meantime, I called my husband immediately-- no answer.
And a lot of my friends were taken to concentration camps.
I was beside myself.
I wanted to know where my husband is.
I called for a whole week.
I couldn't get him.
And one night, I dreamt my husband was taken to Dachau.
So I called my mother-in-law--
No, no, but I dreamt.
I heard the word.
I heard a concentration camp in Dachau, but I didn't know anything about it.
So I called my-- my mother-in-- my mother-in-law.
And she said, Irmie, we just came home.
We were released from prison.
We were all taken to prison.
But at that time, they let the women go.
And we just came home, and Hugo and Father was taken to the concentration camp in Dachau.
And here I am, in Berlin.
I went to the Jewish community and asked them if they could help me get my husband out.
And they said, impossible-- the way he got in, the way he will get out.
In the meantime, a cousin of Hugo's from Frankfurt am Main, who was a museum-- an art curator-- was also taken into a concentration camp.
And he was a very, very affluent man.
He also had his own business.
He, I think, had his own bank.
When he came in, he didn't last long.
He died.
He was tortured.
And I found out that my husband was in the same concentration camp.
I had to work fast.
And when they refused to help me in any way, which I understood-- 6 million Jews were taken into concentration camp.
Where do they start?
What can they do?
So my experience with a Supreme Court lawyer came handy.
I knew how to prepare a brief, and I wrote a brief to the Gestapo in Berlin.
And I got an interview.
And when I went, it was a big building, and the office were downstairs, like in a cellar, like in a dungeon.
And I was told that I will never get my husband out, unless I have a place to go away-- to migrate.
I had some families that my father helped to get into the United States, but they all had closed ears.
Nobody helped us.
We could not get any help.
So what did I do?
I had to do something.
I went to an English agency, travel agency, and I bought two tickets to England.
I knew that I would not be able to go to England unless I had a sponsor, and I didn't have one at the time.
But I had the tickets.
So he said, where are you going to go?
I said, England.
Do you have any proof?
Here are my tickets.
And he took it.
When was this, do you know?
That was-- that was in-- on the-- that was on the 20th of November, because he was taken on the 10th of November.
He was one week in the prison, in Dusseldorf.
It must've been around the 18th to 22nd.
Of November, 1938-- so in the meantime, while I was waiting down in the dungeon, so to speak, on a bench, this man-- this Gestapo man-- let a couple out of his office.
And he threw her out.
She had a German, non-Jewish lawyer.
And he screamed on top of his voice-- on top of his voice.
If you are continuing to present that Jew woman, I will see to it that the Gestapo takes you to a concentration camp.
And I was next.
I prayed to God, I hope I'm not going to be next, because the other gentleman in the other office was an elderly man.
And he looked kind of nice, to me.
But this rough, young Nazi called me in.
And when he asked me where to go, I said England.
So he said, come and sit down.
And I sat down very calmly, and I told him my story.
And he-- after he bought my story, he looked down.
And he said, that's rather strange.
Your husband was not on the list to be taken to a concentration camp.
How come?
I said, he was taken in Dusseldorf.
He said, he visited his parents in Dusseldorf.
He said, I'm sorry.
There's nothing I can do.
You have to go to Dusseldorf.
I had a little bit of a nerve.
And I said, sir, being that you do know my case completely, is it possible that you can refer my case to Dusseldorf?
At I have a little baby, I have to take care of the baby.
I have a job.
I have to make money to supply my baby.
Is that a possibility, that you can do it?
He says, I tell you what.
You come with me.
I have to talk to my supervisor and see what he will have to say.
But when he calls you in, only answer yes and no.
Don't give any talk, and don't say anything.
I have no idea, no idea.
I was very calm and very composed.
I was not panicky, and I think that must have made an impression.
And he took me, then, to the supervisor, and they called me in.
And he said, do you have money?
I asked, what for?
For the trip from Dachau to Dusseldorf.
I said yes.
And that was all, and they released me.
And then, when I came out, I didn't hear.
I didn't hear.
So I called the man, not giving my name.
I only said, I was with you-- and he had lots of people at that time, with him.
He wouldn't know who I was.
And he started screaming, and I hung up.
On the-- oh, I will mention, when-- before I left, I said, my husband has birthday on the 27th of December.
Would there be a possibility for him to come out before then?
That would be his greatest present.
